title: Economic Bulletin Issue 6, 2025
summary: The ECB Governing Council kept its three key interest rates unchanged at its September 11, 2025 meeting, with inflation near the 2% medium-term target. ECB staff projections show headline HICP inflation averaging 2.1% in 2025, 1.7% in 2026, and 1.9% in 2027, while euro area GDP growth is projected at 1.2% in 2025, 1.0% in 2026, and 1.3% in 2027.
key_points: ECB Governing Council held rates unchanged at September 11, 2025 meeting; inflation at around 2% target with broadly unchanged inflation outlook
Headline HICP inflation projected at 2.1% (2025), 1.7% (2026), 1.9% (2027); core HICP excl. energy and food at 2.4% (2025), 1.9% (2026), 1.8% (2027)
Euro area GDP growth revised up to 1.2% in 2025 (from 0.9% June projection), 1.0% in 2026 (down 0.1 pp), 1.3% in 2027 (unchanged)
Unemployment rate at 6.2% in July 2025; labour market remains a source of economic strength
Compensation per employee growth decelerated to 3.9% in Q2 2025 from 4.0% in Q1 and 4.8% in Q2 2024
Governing Council following data-dependent, meeting-by-meeting approach; not pre-committing to particular rate path
New US-EU trade agreement reduced trade policy uncertainty but implies higher tariffs on euro area exports to United States
why_it_matters: This ECB Economic Bulletin provides the central bank's comprehensive assessment of euro area economic conditions and monetary policy rationale. The unchanged rate decision and stable inflation projections signal the ECB is in a cautious hold mode, monitoring whether inflation will decline to target as projected. The upward revision to 2025 GDP growth and downward revision to 2026 growth reflects near-term trade frontloading effects from tariff uncertainty, with underlying euro area momentum remaining positive. Forward guidance emphasizing data-dependence without pre-commitment gives the ECB flexibility to respond to evolving conditions.
macro_context: The ECB projects euro area growth will strengthen later in the projection horizon, supported by rising real wages, employment growth, infrastructure and defence spending (primarily in Germany), less restrictive financing conditions, and a rebound in foreign demand in 2027. Trade tariffs and related uncertainty contributed to strong fluctuations in H1 2025 economic activity with frontloading effects (especially in Ireland). Wage growth moderation is expected to continue, with unit labour cost growth slowing significantly as past real wage losses are recouped and productivity recovers. Energy inflation expected to remain volatile but rise over the projection horizon partly due to EU ETS 2 starting in 2027.
important_numbers: 2.4% core HICP excl. energy and food 2025
1.9% core HICP excl. energy and food 2026
1.8% core HICP excl. energy and food 2027

issues_found: URL contains 'eb202506' which could imply June 2025, but this is likely the reference period designation used by ECB, not the publication date. The content consistently references the September 11, 2025 Governing Council meeting and September 2025 projections.
Source body was truncated at end; however, the key inflation projection revision details were captured in the summary and key_points.

human_review.notes: Published date discrepancy needs clarification: URL indicates Issue 6 (June 2025) but content discusses September 2025 meeting. Also, this publication is historical (approximately 6 months old relative to TODAY), so some projections may have been superseded by subsequent ECB communications.

notes: This is the ECB's Economic Bulletin providing comprehensive analysis of economic and monetary developments. Source discusses both macro catalysts (inflation, GDP, employment data) and monetary policy decisions but does not provide explicit forward-looking market impact statements. The document is a historical record as of TODAY. Confidence slightly lowered due to: (1) publication date uncertainty, (2) truncated source body.
